era_context: Late Bronze Age, with Egypt under Thutmose III at the apex of its imperial power, the Hittite Empire consolidating its position in Anatolia and competing for influence in Syria, Mycenaean Greece flourishing in its palace period with sophisticated administrative and trade systems, and the Shang Dynasty maintaining control over China. This was an era of international trade, diplomatic correspondence, and military competition between major powers.

events.title: Thutmose III's reign at its height — Egyptian imperial dominance
events.description: Thutmose III was at the height of his power and influence, having consolidated Egyptian control over vast territories from Nubia to Syria. His reign represented the apex of New Kingdom Egyptian imperial expansion. The year -1395 falls within this period of maximum Egyptian dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. His military innovations, administrative reforms, and diplomatic acumen made him one of the most significant pharaohs in Egyptian history.
key_figures: Thutmose III (Pharaoh of Egypt, r. c. -1479 to -1425 BCE)

sources.name: Karnak Temple inscriptions documenting Thutmose III's campaigns and achievements
Egyptian administrative records and king lists

events.certainty_note: Thutmose III's reign is well-attested and dated to c. -1479 to -1425 BCE by conventional chronology. The year -1395 falls securely within his reign, though specific events for this individual year are not documented.

events.title: Hittite Empire consolidation and diplomatic activities
events.description: The Hittite Empire was consolidating its position in Anatolia and engaging in diplomatic and military activities to maintain its sphere of influence in Syria and the Levant. The Hittites were developing sophisticated administrative and military systems that would later make them a major power in the region. The year -1395 falls within this period of Hittite consolidation.
events.certainty_note: Hittite political activities during this period are known from archaeological evidence, but specific events in -1395 are not individually documented.

events.title: Mycenaean palace civilization at its height
events.description: Mycenaean civilization was flourishing in its palace period, with major centers at Mycenae, Pylos, and Tiryns serving as centers of political, economic, and religious authority. The palaces were engaged in extensive trade networks throughout the Eastern Mediterranean, exchanging Greek pottery, olive oil, and wine for tin, cedar, and other luxury goods. Linear B script was in use for administrative purposes. The year -1395 falls within the height of Mycenaean palace culture.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Mycenaean palace sites
Linear B tablets from Pylos and Knossos

events.certainty_note: Mycenaean palace culture is well-attested archaeologically (c. -1600 to -1100 BCE), with -1395 falling within the height of this period. Specific events for this year are not documented.

events.title: Shang Dynasty in China — oracle bone divination and bronze technology
events.description: The Shang Dynasty was in its mature phase, with the capital at Anyang serving as the center of political, religious, and economic authority. Oracle bone divination was a central practice of Shang governance, addressing questions of warfare, agriculture, weather, and succession. Bronze technology was highly developed, with the Shang producing sophisticated weapons and ritual vessels. The year -1395 falls within the well-documented Shang period.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of Shang bronze artifacts
Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The Shang Dynasty's existence and practices are well-attested, but precise dating of individual years remains uncertain due to ongoing chronological debates among scholars. The conventional dating places the Shang c. -1600 to -1046 BCE.

events.title: International trade networks connecting Eastern Mediterranean and Near East
events.region: Eastern Mediterranean, Near East, Egypt, Levant, Anatolia, Aegean
events.description: The year -1395 falls within a period of extensive international trade networks connecting Egypt, the Levant, Anatolia, and the Aegean. Tin from Central Asia and Afghanistan was traded along routes that passed through the Levant and Anatolia to reach Egypt and the Aegean. Cedar from Lebanon was highly valued in Egypt for shipbuilding. Mycenaean pottery and olive oil were traded throughout the Eastern Mediterranean. These trade networks facilitated not only economic exchange but also cultural and technological transfer between civilizations.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of trade goods in excavated sites across the Eastern Mediterranean

events.certainty_note: International trade networks during the Late Bronze Age are well-attested archaeologically, though specific transactions in -1395 are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional chronology for the Late Bronze Age, particularly the dating of Thutmose III and other Egyptian pharaohs, has been subject to significant scholarly debate. Some scholars, particularly advocates of the 'New Chronology' (associated with scholars like David Rohl), propose substantially different dates for Egyptian rulers, which would shift the dating of -1395 and related events by decades or even centuries. Additionally, the precise synchronization between Egyptian, Hittite, Mycenaean, and Chinese chronologies remains contested, with some scholars proposing alternative correlations. The significance attributed to Thutmose III's reign as the 'apex' of Egyptian power is also debated; some scholars emphasize the later 19th Dynasty as equally or more significant. Furthermore, the extent of Mycenaean trade and the nature of Mycenaean-Minoan relations are subjects of ongoing scholarly discussion, with some scholars questioning the degree of Mycenaean dominance over Minoan Crete during this period.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -1395 is heavily dependent on Egyptian administrative records and temple inscriptions, which are biased toward elite political and military narratives and the glorification of pharaonic achievements. Archaeological evidence provides material context but rarely documents specific events for individual years. The Hittite record is similarly biased toward royal narratives. The Mycenaean and Shang records are limited in their ability to pinpoint events to a single year. The absence of contemporary written records from most regions means that the 'history' of -1395 is largely a reconstruction based on broader period contexts rather than year-specific documentation. Chronological uncertainty is substantial, particularly for non-Egyptian civilizations, and the conventional dating framework itself is contested by some scholars. The historical record is also heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East; the history of other regions (Africa, Europe, Asia, the Americas) during this year is largely unknown or inferred from archaeological evidence.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Thutmose III's reign spanning the entire period -1399 to -1395
Hittite and Egyptian activities were concurrent and competitive during this period
Mycenaean civilization was a key participant in international trade networks
Trade networks connected all major civilizations of the period
